basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Qāriʿa — “The Calamity”, “that which strikes” — is one of the names of the Day of Judgment. The root q-r-ʿ names the deafening sound of a violent blow: the door beaten in, the bursting drum. But the Sufi reading does not wait for the last day to hear the qāriʿa. Tustarī distinguishes three blows: “The blow is threefold — the one that strikes the bodies is the arrow of death; the one that strikes the deeds is God's questioning of them; and the one that strikes the hearts is the fear of severance (qaṭīʿa, القَطِيعَة) — the dread of being cut off from Him.” Three blows that may touch the soul before the final hour. And Qushayrī, on the mountains that become like carded wool, gives the point: “Those who laid claim to something here, the lords of power — at the Resurrection, when they are raised, they will be weaker than every weak thing. For there, powers fall, and claims are annulled.” The sūra is therefore first a dismantling: what seemed unshakable is revealed light as wool. And only the weight that the deeds will have had in the Balance remains.

versets.commentaire: A single word — set down. Qushayrī: “al-qāriʿa is one of the names of the Resurrection; the form ‘fāʿila' of qarʿ, which means to strike with violence. It is so named because it strikes them.” The word is itself a blow; it begins before the sense begins.

mots.sens: the Calamity, the one that strikes
mots.def: qāriʿa: feminine active participle of qaraʿa (to strike with deafening sound); one of the names of the Day of Judgment.

versets.commentaire: The question takes the name back up — like an echo that prolongs the blow. The sūra, by this device, lets the name go on sounding in the ear before the mind takes hold of it. Thus, Tustarī suggests implicitly, the first way the qāriʿa reaches us is already by the sound that resounds.

versets.trad: And what shall apprise you of what the Calamity is?
versets.commentaire: Tustarī gives here a rule for reading the Quran: “Everything in the Quran preceded by the words mā adrāka (what shall apprise you) is something about which He had not yet given information.” It is a formula of reserve: what follows does not define — it evokes. The great Blow, in itself, exceeds what one can say.

mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: A Day when mankind shall be like scattered moths,
versets.commentaire: al-farāsh al-mabthūth (الفَرَاش المَبْثُوث): little winged insects scattered. Tustarī adds the key: “They will bump into each other because of the awe (hayba, هَيْبَة) they feel before God.” Disorder is not absence of direction — it is the soul caught in too great a Presence for it to be able to bear its silence. Before the Face, habits come undone.

mots.def: yawm: day; construct accusative, introduces the temporal circumstance.
kāna: to be; imperfect, future sense.
farāsh: night moths, winged insects drawn to light.
mabthūth: passive participle of bathth (to scatter); spread out, dispersed.

versets.trad: and the mountains shall be like carded wool.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī gives the spiritual key: “The meaning is that those who laid claim and those who held power here below, on the Day of Resurrection, become weaker than every weak thing — for there, powers fall, and claims are annulled.” Mountains, in the Quran, image the great solid things, the stable powers. And they become like carded wool — light, dispersed by the slightest breath. All that claimed solidity is revealed to be wool.

mots.def: kāna: to be; in the feminine imperfect (jibāl is a feminine plural).
jabal: mountain; plural jibāl. Figure of the solid, the lasting.
manfūsh: passive participle of nafasha (to card wool).

versets.trad: As for one whose scales are heavy,
versets.commentaire: thaqulat mawāzīnuhu: his scales have weighed heavy. Qushayrī adds a nuance touching the mystery: “The weighing of deeds, on that Day, is by the weight of the pages. And it is said: in place of each part of his deeds, a substance (jawhar, جَوْهَر) is created — and these substances are weighed. That is the weighing of deeds.” Deeds are not weighed in themselves — they are first materialised into spiritual substances, and it is these substances that make the weight. A striking image: the soul will see, on the Day, the condensation of all that it has done, in forms it had not recognised but which are, in truth, what it has woven.

mots.def: thaqula: to be heavy, to weigh.
mawāzīn: plural of mīzān, the balance; here the weighings of each act.

versets.trad: he shall enjoy a life that contents,
versets.commentaire: ʿīsha rāḍiya: a life that contents — or: a life that contents itself. The active participle (rāḍiya, “that is content”) gives life itself the quality of contentment. In the Sufi reading: it is not a life that pleases the subject (the subject is no longer separate from his life); it is a life become, in its very substance, contentment. A state surpassing pleasure, answering at another level to the riḍāʾ (the mutual satisfaction between God and the servant) found at the end of al-Bayyina.

mots.def: rāḍiya: feminine active participle of raḍiya (to be content); literally “that is content” or “that contents”.

versets.trad: but as for one whose scales are light,
versets.commentaire: Inverse symmetry. Khaffat: they have been light. When little substance has crystallised from the deeds — when few acts in God have entered the weighing — the scales do not tip. What made weight in the world here below makes no weight there.

mots.def: khaffa: to be light, to weigh little.

versets.trad: an abyss shall be his refuge.
versets.commentaire: ummuhu hāwiya — literally, “his mother is the Abyss”. Umm (mother) comes from the verb amma (to direct oneself to), hence the reading: that to which he goes like a child. The image is harsh: the place that should have been maternal tenderness has become the Abyss. That is to say: what the soul was turning toward — its claims, its powers, its goods — draws it in the end into the void.

mots.def: umm: mother; by extension, that toward which one goes (from the verb amma).
hāwiya: feminine active participle of hawā (to fall); that which falls or makes one fall. A name of the Fire.

versets.trad: And what shall apprise you of what that is?
versets.commentaire: The same formula as for the great Blow. Once again, language lays down its arms before what it evokes. The final hāʾ of pause — hiyah — lets the voice fall back, as if the sentence itself fell into its own emptiness.

mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: hiya: feminine personal pronoun; with final hāʾ of pause.

versets.commentaire: nār ḥāmiya: a fire of extreme heat. The sūra ends in two words — like the blow it announced: sharp, without gloss, without addition. But Qushayrī adds, in the margin, a precious nuance: “He did not say that the states will be weighed, only the acts. But each is recompensed by his state, in so far as that state is his earning (kasb) — or in so far as he set himself on the way by its causes.” The balance forgets nothing.

mots.def: ḥāmiya: feminine active participle of ḥamiya (to be burning); a fire of extreme heat.
